entries.text: Suggested subject: Three Things to Know — 17 March 2026 Disruption Watch 17 March 2026 Three disruptive tech signals worth two minutes of your morning. 1. Nvidia doubles its AI chip forecast to $1 trillion — and says the inference era has arrived Jensen Huang told 30,000 GTC attendees that Nvidia now expects "at least $1 trillion" in AI chip revenue through 2027, up from $500 billion — the largest upward revision in semiconductor history. The driver is the shift from AI training to inference: AI doing productive work at scale, not just learning. Andreessen Horowitz separately argued that AI agents will sit on top of legacy enterprise software like SAP and Oracle, threatening the $380 billion systems integrator market. Why it matters: The inference pivot changes the economics of enterprise AI from "experimentation budget" to "operational infrastructure." If agents can replace implementation consultants, every large IT transformation programme is repriced. Source: Nvidia GTC 2026 2. Kraken becomes the first crypto company to get a Federal Reserve master account Kraken Financial received direct access to Fedwire — the interbank payment system that moves trillions daily — making it the first digital asset firm with Fed payment rails. The application took five years. In the same week, Galaxy Digital launched a CLO on Avalanche (structured credit on a public blockchain), and both Wells Fargo and SoFi are developing stablecoin strategies. Why it matters: The wall between crypto infrastructure and traditional finance has been formally breached. This creates precedent pressure on the FCA and opens a path for digital asset firms to compete directly on payment speed and cost. Source: Kraken Blog 3. First humanoid robot deployed to an active battlefield — and the next version ships in April Foundation's Phantom MK-1 is conducting reconnaissance on the Ukrainian front line — the first humanoid robot in combat. It can operate small arms and its battlefield data is feeding directly into the MK-2, due in April. Separately, Beijing hosted a robot half marathon and Niantic revealed that 30 billion Pok mon Go images are being used to train robotic navigation systems. Why it matters: The iteration speed — from first deployment to next-generation in weeks — compresses every timeline for humanoid robots entering commercial environments. Gaming data quietly becoming industrial training data is a business model most analysts haven't modelled. Source: Robotics Automation News Full briefing: Issue #17 available in the Disruption Watch hub.

entries.headline: Three Things to Know — 18 March 2026
entries.text: Subject: Three Things to Know — 18 March 2026 Disruption Watch: Three Things to Know 1. Mastercard Bets $1.8B on Stablecoin Rails Mastercard announced it will acquire BVNK, a stablecoin infrastructure provider, for up to $1.8 billion. The deal connects on-chain payments with traditional fiat rails. With Visa simultaneously launching agentic payments in Europe and the stablecoin market now at $300 billion, the convergence of traditional and crypto finance is accelerating. Why this matters: The payment giants are no longer experimenting — they're acquiring the infrastructure to run hybrid finance at scale. Source: Mastercard Acquiring Stablecoin Startup BVNK 2. Nvidia Declares the Agent Economy Open at GTC At GTC 2026, Jensen Huang launched NemoClaw (security guardrails for AI agents), the Vera Rubin chip platform, and announced a $27B infrastructure deal with Meta via Nebius. Meanwhile, Ramp and Stripe are racing to give AI agents their own payment cards with built-in spending controls. Why this matters: The infrastructure for autonomous AI commerce — agents that can transact, book, purchase, and operate without human intervention — is being built this week. Source: GTC 2026 News 3. Financial Crime Hits $4.4 Trillion — Growing Faster Than Most Economies Nasdaq Verafin's 2026 report puts global illicit financial activity at $4.4 trillion, compounding at 19.2% annually. AI-driven attacks are the fastest-growing category, with 90% of financial crime professionals reporting increases. Why this matters: Financial crime is now roughly half the size of the global oil industry, and traditional fraud detection is blind to agent-initiated transactions. Source: Nasdaq Verafin Global Financial Crime Report Full briefing: Issue #18 available in the Disruption Watch hub | Methodology .

Subject: Three Things to Know 19 March 2026 Three Things to Know 19 March 2026 Disruption Watch 1. The internet finally has a payment layer for machines. Stripe-backed Tempo launched the Machine Payments Protocol (MPP), an open-source, HTTP-native protocol that lets AI agents send and receive payments across fiat and crypto rails without human intervention. Visa contributed card payment specifications; Stripe extended to additional rails. The protocol fills a gap reserved in HTTP since 1995 (status code 402: "Payment Required") and is targeting IETF standardisation. Why this matters: If MPP achieves adoption, every API becomes monetisable and every agent becomes a customer. The advertising model of the web faces its first credible structural alternative. Fortune 2. Iran struck Qatar's largest LNG facility. Oil surged 6%. Iran launched missile strikes on Qatar's Ras Laffan Industrial City, the world's largest LNG export hub. Brent crude jumped 6.3% to $114.13 per barrel. Dutch natural gas benchmarks spiked 25%. Qatar had already suspended LNG production after earlier attacks. Europe's post-Russia energy diversification strategy now faces a direct supply threat. Why this matters: Energy security assumptions are being tested in real time. Organisations with European energy exposure, commodity dependencies, or shipping through Hormuz need to scenario-plan for sustained disruption. CNBC 3. Karpathy's AI agents ran 700 research experiments in two days, autonomously. Andrej Karpathy released "autoresearch," an open-source system where AI agents autonomously design, run, and evaluate training experiments. In its first run, the system executed 700 experiments and discovered 20 optimisations that delivered an 11% speedup on larger models. Karpathy predicts all frontier labs will adopt this approach. Why this matters: Research velocity at AI labs is about to increase dramatically. The organisations that adopt autonomous research loops first will compound advantages in model performance and cost reduction. Fortune Full briefing: Issue #19 available in the Disruption Watch hub.

entries.headline: Three Things to Know - 20 March 2026
entries.text: Disruption Watch Three Things to Know | 20 March 2026 1. Visa, Stripe, and Mastercard are building payment rails for AI agents and stablecoins simultaneously Visa launched CLI, enabling AI agents to initiate card payments autonomously. The same week, Stripe's Tempo blockchain went live with a Machine Payments Protocol for agent-to-service transactions, and Mastercard committed up to $1.8 billion to acquire stablecoin infrastructure firm BVNK. Three competing payment stacks, all designed for non-human transactors, launched within 48 hours of each other. Why it matters: The payment networks are no longer debating whether AI agents and stablecoins will reshape commerce; they are racing to own the infrastructure layer. Banks that do not have a position on agent-compatible payment rails will be building one reactively within 18 months. PYMNTS: Visa Teams With Stripe on Agent Payments 2. Cursor's in-house coding model outperforms Opus 4.6 at one twentieth of the cost Cursor shipped Composer 2, claiming 61.7% on Terminal-Bench 2.0 versus 58% for Anthropic's Opus 4.6, at roughly 5% of the price per token. Separately, MiniMax released M2.7, a model that used earlier versions of itself to write its own training code across 100+ refinement cycles. Application-layer companies are now building competitive models optimised for their specific workflows. Why it matters: The AI model landscape is fragmenting from "one general model" to "many specialist models." AI procurement decisions increasingly require evaluating domain-specific options rather than defaulting to the largest provider. Cursor Blog: Composer 2 3. The world's first autonomy-first copper mine is now operational in Utah a16z profiled Copper One, operated by Mariana Minerals using a unified AI platform (MarianaOS) that combines reinforcement learning, autonomous fleet orchestration, and process plant control. The mine targets 50,000 metric tonnes of annual output from both geological deposits and domestic scrap recycling, whilst US refined copper output has fallen 50% in 25 years and 200,000 mining workers will retire this decade. Why it matters: AI is entering primary resource extraction, not just digital workflows. The materials bottleneck for electrification and data centre expansion is structural, and autonomous mining may be the only scalable response to a workforce that is physically disappearing. a16z: Copper One Full briefing: Issue #21 available in the Disruption Watch hub.

entries.headline: Three Things to Know - 21 March 2026
entries.text: Subject: Three Things to Know - 21 March 2026 Three Things to Know 1 Cursor Composer 2 Beats Opus 4.6 on Terminal-Bench Cursor's in-house model scored 61.7% versus Anthropic's Opus 4.6 at 58% on Terminal-Bench 2.0. Cursor now generates £1.55B ARR and has become the first coding tool to build an AI model that outperforms its own supplier. The traditional model moat is dissolving. Why this matters: If application-layer companies can build competitive foundation models for their vertical, the current AI supply chain may invert. Every bank procuring AI as a service should be watching this closely. Cursor.com 2 OpenAI Acquires Astral for Developer Stack Control OpenAI purchased Astral (makers of Ruff and uv), signalling that AI labs are moving from pure model provision to full-stack developer platforms. The acquisition costs approximately £371M at current rates and marks a significant vertical integration play. Why this matters: The battle for developer workflow is accelerating. Banks with large internal development teams now face new platform lock-in risk as AI labs vertically integrate across the entire toolchain. OpenAI announcements 3 Google Stitch Generates Navigable 3D Worlds from Single Images Google DeepMind released Stitch, which can generate fully navigable 3D environments from a single photograph. Combined with Apple's new 4K spatial display, the interface layer for spatial computing is advancing faster than anticipated. Why this matters: The convergence of generative AI and spatial computing will reshape how customers interact with financial services within 3-5 years. Branch design, wealth advisory, and product visualisation are all in scope for reimagining. Google DeepMind research Full briefing: Issue #22 available in the Disruption Watch hub. | Methodology

entries.headline: Three Things to Know — 23 March 2026
entries.text: Subject: Three Things to Know, 23 March 2026 Disruption Watch: Three Things to Know 1. Private Credit Meets AI: BlackRock Gates £620M in Redemptions BlackRock's HPS Corporate Lending Fund capped redemptions at 5% after investors requested 9.3%, the first time limits have been breached. Blackstone injected £310 million into BCRED to stabilise outflows. The trigger: AI coding tools are eroding the value of software companies that collateralise £19.4 billion in private loans, with JPMorgan marking down software collateral. Why this matters: Technology disruption is now transmitting through credit markets in ways traditional risk models do not capture. Any lending exposure to software-collateralised assets warrants urgent review. Source: Bloomberg 2. NVIDIA's Groq Chip Claims 35x Throughput: The Inference Economy Arrives The first chip from NVIDIA's £15.5 billion Groq acquisition ships in Q3, claiming 35 times higher throughput per megawatt than current Blackwell systems. Cursor's AI coding tool doubled revenue to £1.55 billion ARR in three months. Andrej Karpathy reports 80% of his coding is now done by AI agents. Why this matters: Inference costs are collapsing, making agentic AI systems economically viable at enterprise scale. Every AI business case built on current pricing assumptions needs revisiting. Source: Next Platform 3. SEC and CFTC Classify 16 Cryptocurrencies as Digital Commodities A joint 68-page interpretation names Bitcoin, Ethereum, Solana, and 13 others as digital commodities regulated by the CFTC, not the SEC. The FCA simultaneously opened a permissions gateway for UK crypto firms from September 2026. Why this matters: Dual-jurisdiction regulatory clarity creates a narrow window for banks to establish crypto custody and tokenisation capabilities before competitors. Source: SEC/CFTC Joint Interpretation Full briefing: Issue #23 available in the Disruption Watch hub | Methodology .

entries.headline: Three Things to Know - 24 March 2026
entries.text: Subject: Three Things to Know, 24 March 2026 Disruption Watch: Three Things to Know 1. Helium Crisis Threatens AI Chip Supply Chain Iran's strike on Ras Laffan, Qatar's largest helium facility, has cut 14% of global helium exports for years. South Korea, which sourced 64% of its helium from Qatar, produces the majority of the world's high bandwidth memory used in AI accelerator chips. Memory prices have already tripled since mid-2025. There is no synthetic substitute for helium, and liquid helium is only viable for 35 to 48 days in storage. Why this matters: Every AI infrastructure investment ultimately depends on semiconductor fabrication. This is a supply chain vulnerability that most digital strategies do not account for. Source: Al Jazeera 2. Anthropic Revenue Doubles in Three Months to USD 19 Billion Anthropic's annualised revenue surged from roughly USD 9 billion at the start of 2026 to USD 19 billion by early March, with USD 6 billion added in February alone. Claude Code contributed USD 2.5 billion annualised. The company is now valued at USD 380 billion following its Series G round. Why this matters: This is the fastest revenue acceleration in enterprise AI history and validates the thesis that agent-based AI tools are reaching mainstream adoption at scale. Source: Yahoo Finance 3. Musk Launches USD 25 Billion Terafab for 1 Terawatt of AI Compute Elon Musk formally launched Terafab, a joint venture between Tesla, SpaceX, and xAI targeting 1 terawatt of annual AI compute, roughly 50 times current global output. 80% of capacity is destined for orbital deployment via Starship. Small-batch production is expected this year. Why this matters: If viable, orbital compute would fundamentally change the economics of AI infrastructure by exploiting constant solar power and vacuum cooling. Source: Tom's Hardware Full briefing: Issue #24 available in the Disruption Watch hub | Methodology .

entries.headline: Three Things to Know - 25 March 2026
entries.text: Subject: Three Things to Know - 25 March 2026 Three Things to Know 25 March 2026 1. Mastercard and Santander Complete Europe's First Live AI Agent Payment Santander and Mastercard executed Europe's first end-to-end payment completed by an AI agent, deployed within Mastercard Agent Pay on a regulated banking framework. The agent autonomously searched options, booked a service, and processed payment without human intervention. Mastercard has separately deployed South Korea's first live agentic transaction for airport car services. Why it matters: Agentic payments have moved from concept to production. Financial institutions without agent authentication and payment frameworks are now operationally behind a live benchmark. Read more 2. Langflow AI Agent Platform Exploited Within 20 Hours of Vulnerability Disclosure A critical vulnerability (CVE-2026-33017, CVSS 9.3) in Langflow, a widely-used open source AI agent orchestration platform, was actively exploited within 20 hours of public disclosure. Attackers required no proof-of-concept code, building functional exploits directly from the security advisory. Compromised instances leaked API keys for OpenAI, Anthropic, AWS, and connected databases. Why it matters: AI agent infrastructure has become a high-value attack vector. Organisations deploying agent orchestration tools must apply equivalent security discipline to core banking systems. Read more 3. Microsoft Down 21% YTD as Copilot Stalls at 3.3% Enterprise Penetration Microsoft's share price has declined 21% year to date, making it the weakest performer among the Magnificent Seven technology stocks. Copilot adoption has reached 15 million seats against a 450 million installed base, representing only 3.3% penetration. A notable leadership exodus has followed, with senior executives moving to Anthropic and other AI-native organisations. Why it matters: The world's largest enterprise software company is struggling to monetise AI at scale. This demonstrates that enterprise AI adoption is more challenging than prevailing narratives suggest, with significant implications for every large organisation's AI investment thesis. Read more Full briefing: Issue #25 available in the Disruption Watch hub.

entries.headline: Three Things to Know, 26 March 2026
entries.text: Three Things to Know 26 March 2026 1. FCA considers rewriting payments rules for AI agents The Financial Conduct Authority's 2026 Payments Regulatory Priorities report flags "agentic AI payments" as potentially requiring regulatory change. This is not theoretical: Mastercard's agentic payment rails are already live across 17+ banks in Latin America, and Starling Bank has launched the UK's first agentic AI financial assistant. The questions now: who provides consent when an AI agent initiates a payment, and who bears liability when it errs? Why this matters: The rules governing autonomous payment agents in the UK will be written in the next 12 to 18 months. Early engagement shapes the framework. 2. Norway's GBP1.5 trillion fund deploys Claude AI at scale The world's largest sovereign wealth fund has deployed Anthropic's Claude across 600+ of its 700 employees, reporting 20% weekly time savings on investment analysis. Staff code their own AI tools to monitor 7,000 companies for ESG and financial risk. The fund is now preparing to let AI agents make limited investment decisions autonomously. Why this matters: When a GBP1.5 trillion institution trusts AI agents with autonomous decision-making, it sets a precedent for asset management, risk, and compliance across the industry. 3. Monument Bank tokenises GBP250M in retail deposits on public blockchain Monument Bank will tokenise up to GBP250 million of retail deposits on the Midnight blockchain (linked to Cardano) in a UK first. Deposits remain FSCS-protected, interest-bearing, and redeemable in sterling. Later phases add tokenised investment products and lending against holdings. The model will be offered via Banking-as-a-Service. Why this matters: This is the first credible test of whether tokenised deposits can coexist with FSCS protection and mainstream banking. If it works, larger institutions will face questions about deposit programmability. Full briefing: Issue #26 available in the Disruption Watch hub. This content is for internal use only.

entries.headline: Three Things to Know - 27 March 2026
entries.text: Three Things to Know 27 March 2026 1. Lloyds Deploys Agentic AI Across 21 Million Accounts Lloyds Banking Group has begun rolling out agentic AI across its entire 21 million customer base, targeting GBP 100M+ in value extraction during 2026. Simultaneously, Visa launched its "Agentic Ready" programme, recruiting Barclays, HSBC UK and Nationwide for autonomous payment trials. Starling Bank also launched the UK's first agentic AI financial assistant. Why this matters: The UK's largest incumbents are moving in parallel on agentic AI, compressing what was expected to be a multi-year adoption curve into months. Source: FFNews 2. Token-Backed Mortgages Enter US Government Framework Better.com and Coinbase launched the first conforming mortgage backed by Bitcoin and USDC collateral, approved within Fannie Mae's GSE framework. Borrowers can pledge crypto assets as collateral without liquidation, maintaining exposure to price appreciation. Why this matters: If on-chain collateral satisfies government-sponsored enterprise standards, it opens pathways for crypto-backed auto loans, personal credit, and eventually UK equivalents. Source: CoinDesk 3. Harvey AI Raises GBP 149M at GBP 8.2B Valuation as Legal AI Market Crystallises Harvey AI closed a GBP 149M funding round at a GBP 8.2B valuation, with annual recurring revenue of GBP 141M as of January 2026. The round was led by Sequoia and positions Harvey as the dominant vertical AI player in legal services. Why this matters: Harvey's trajectory shows vertical AI companies can reach meaningful scale faster than horizontal platforms, a pattern that will extend to financial services. Source: AI Secret newsletter Full briefing: Issue #27 available in the Disruption Watch hub.

entries.headline: Three Things to Know | 28 March 2026
entries.text: The 60-Second Brief Three Things to Know Today 28 March 2026 1 Anthropic Targets October IPO as AI Lab Revenue Hits £11B Anthropic is in early discussions for a potential IPO as soon as October 2026, according to The Information. The company closed a £23.8B ($30B) funding round in February at a £301B ($380B) valuation, and is generating approximately £11.1B ($14B) in annualised revenue. Goldman Sachs, JPMorgan, and Morgan Stanley are amongst the banks in preliminary talks. Why this matters When your AI vendor is worth more than most banks and heading for public markets, the procurement relationship changes fundamentally. The UK Treasury Committee has separately recommended designating major AI providers as "critical third parties" for financial services, meaning Anthropic's corporate trajectory is now a regulatory concern, not just a technology one. 2 Karpathy's AI Agent Ran 700 Experiments in 48 Hours Andrej Karpathy released Autoresearch, a project where an AI coding agent autonomously conducted 700 training experiments over two days, discovering 20 optimisations for improving language model training. The project attracted 8.6 million views on X within 48 hours. Karpathy now argues that "humans no longer write most code" and that we have entered the era of agentic engineering. Why this matters If autonomous research agents can discover optimisations that human researchers miss, the competitive advantage shifts from having the best team to having the best agent infrastructure. Financial services R&D, risk modelling, and quantitative strategy teams should be watching this closely. 3 Claude Chrome Extension Vulnerability Exposes AI Agent Attack Surface A vulnerability was disclosed in Anthropic's Claude Chrome Extension that allows any website to silently inject prompts into the AI assistant. Three additional vulnerabilities in LangChain and LangGraph were simultaneously disclosed, exposing filesystem data, environment secrets, and conversation history. Separately, a Trivy supply chain attack spread a CanisterWorm across 47 npm packages. Why this matters As banks accelerate deployment of AI agents, the attack surface extends beyond the model into browser extensions, orchestration frameworks, and integration layers. These disclosures confirm that the middleware powering agentic workflows is not yet battle-hardened, and banks should audit every layer of their agent stack, not just the model provider. Read the full Daily Briefing This content is for internal use only.

entries.headline: Three Things to Know - 29 March 2026
entries.text: Three Things to Know 29 March 2026 1. Banks Scored 5% in the Guardian Agent War Fintech Brainfood's fourth "Wallet Wars" analysis maps who gets to own personal finance when wallets become AI agents. Simon Taylor scored seven categories of contenders against six requirements (identity, payments, authentication, data access, AI capability, trust). Banks scored 5%, the lowest of all. Apple led at 25%, followed by new startups (20%), AI Labs and Open Source (15% each). The structural gap: banks have identity and deposits but lack AI capability and cross-silo data access. New startups like Natural (payments accounts for agents), Pay AI (biometric agent approval), and Era (personal MCP for bank accounts) are building the infrastructure layer that could make banks into mere plumbing. Why this matters: The infrastructure stack for agentic finance is being built around banks, not by them. If startups own the agent-wallet interface layer, banks become commodity payment rails. Source: Fintech Brainfood 2. Tokenisation Crossed the Infrastructure Threshold In a single week, NYSE named Securitize as its first digital transfer agent for blockchain-native securities, Franklin Templeton (£1.3T AUM) partnered with Ondo to tokenise five ETFs, Mastercard agreed to acquire stablecoin startup BVNK for up to £1.37B ($1.80B), and Tether signed a Big Four firm for its first-ever full reserve audit covering £140B ($184B) in USDT. Three of the top five ETF managers now have tokenised products. Meanwhile, the stablecoin yield compromise in the CLARITY Act raises deposit flight concerns for traditional banks. Why this matters: Tokenised infrastructure no longer awaits regulatory permission. Tier-1 incumbents (NYSE, Franklin Templeton, Mastercard) have joined issuers and stablecoin operators in moving live. Deposits flowing into stablecoins accelerates this shift. Source: Industry announcements 3. Hormuz Crisis Enters Second Month with Record Oil Surge Brent crude is heading for a record monthly surge of over 55% in March, with the Strait of Hormuz closure now in its second month. The world has lost 4.5 to 5 million barrels per day of supply (about 5% of global output). Pakistan hosted Saudi Arabia, Egypt, and Turkey on 29 March to discuss reopening, while Iran introduced a new demand: a toll on Hormuz shipping transit. For banks, the three immediate effects are corporate loan book stress in energy-intensive sectors, consumer mortgage affordability pressure from inflation expectations, and potential upside in energy trading desks. Why this matters: A month into the closure, the energy market is showing structural stress. If Iran secures a transit toll or the blockade extends, this becomes a permanent change to global oil economics, not a temporary supply shock. Source: Energy and geopolitical sources Full briefing: Issue #29 available in the Disruption Watch hub.

entries.headline: Three Things to Know Today
entries.text: Disruption Watch (beta) Foresight Engine Radar Search Three Things to Know The 60-Second Brief Three Things to Know Today 31 March 2026 1 min read 1 China's Open-Source AI Flywheel Creates Structural Advantage the US Cannot Match With Closed Models The USCC's "Two Loops" report warns that China's willingness to publish model weights is accelerating both innovation and deployment simultaneously. Good-enough open models are being embedded into manufacturing, drones, and robots, creating a data flywheel that trains better models. The dual-growth approach (frontier models plus mass deployment) is fundamentally different from the US closed-model strategy. Meanwhile, Anthropic is rationing inference capacity through peak-hour pricing, signalling demand exceeding supply for frontier AI. Why this matters Access to compute, not model quality, is becoming the binding constraint. China's mass-deployment strategy creates a workforce that learns by doing at national scale, whilst US capacity is concentrated in a handful of frontier labs. Banks planning AI integration must consider which ecosystem they are building on, and whether inference rationing will constrain their deployment timelines. 2 Anthropic Reaches 380B Valuation on 19B ARR as AI Capital Creation Hits 13 Trillion Anthropic closed a 27.3 billion Series G at a 380 billion valuation. Coatue's financial model projects 1.51 trillion valuation by 2030, with 14 billion loss on 18 billion revenue in 2026, ramping to 48 billion EBITDA on 200 billion revenue by 2031. AI-related market capitalisation has grown by approximately 13 trillion in 1,216 days since ChatGPT launched, roughly 10 times the rate of internet-era capital creation after the Netscape IPO. Why this matters The scale of capital reallocation toward AI is unprecedented. Anthropic's projections imply market confidence in 200B+ revenue potential within five years. Capital markets are voting for disruption faster than regulation can respond. Banks face a dual challenge: their own AI investment must accelerate whilst the companies disrupting them attract capital at rates that dwarf incumbent market caps. 3 DTCC Tokenises Treasuries on Canton Network as NYSE Launches 24/7 Trading The DTCC received an SEC No-Action Letter to tokenise US Treasury securities on the Canton Network. NYSE partnered with Securitize for round-the-clock tokenised trading. The ECB's DLT collateral framework went live. The real-world assets market has reached approximately GBP 27 billion. This is not experimentation. Institutional plumbing for tokenised finance is now being built by the incumbents themselves, not by crypto-native startups. Why this matters When the deposit gatekeepers and the clearing houses start building infrastructure for tokenised assets, you are witnessing institutional adoption, not speculation. The implications for market structure, settlement, and custody are profound. Banks and asset managers must now integrate tokenised workflows into core operatin

entries.headline: Three Things to Know Today
entries.text: Disruption Watch (beta) Foresight Engine Radar Search Three Things to Know The 60-Second Brief Three Things to Know Today 1 April 2026 1 min read 1 Iran Conflict Opens Digital Front as IRGC Names 18 US Tech Companies as Targets The IRGC released a statement threatening to target 18 US tech and defence companies including Cisco, HP, Intel, Oracle, Microsoft, Apple, Google, Meta, IBM, Dell, Palantir, Nvidia, Tesla, and Boeing. Iranian drone strikes have already incapacitated AWS cloud computing facilities in Bahrain and the UAE. This represents a new escalation; the conflict moving from oil and maritime chokepoints to targeting the global technology backbone. Why this matters For any institution dependent on cloud infrastructure in the Gulf region, this is no longer a theoretical risk. Banks should stress-test cloud infrastructure resilience against state-level targeting. The targeting of 18 named companies creates immediate supply chain and operational continuity questions across every sector that depends on their services. 2 Anthropic's "Generational Run" Inverts the AI Revenue Model as Enterprise Overtakes Consumer Anthropic is described as being on a "generational run" with an enterprise revenue structure inverted versus OpenAI: 75% API/enterprise versus OpenAI's 75% consumer subscriptions. Multi-agent coordination is emerging rapidly, with OpenAI releasing a Codex plugin for Claude Code and OpenClaw enabling cross-system agent orchestration. Ethan Mollick argues the AI interface problem is the key adoption blocker: research shows chatbots create cognitive overload that offsets productivity gains. Mastercard completed the first live agentic transaction in Hong Kong with HSBC and DBS. Why this matters The interface, not the model, is becoming the binding constraint on AI adoption. Anthropic's enterprise-first revenue structure looks more durable than consumer subscription dependency, especially as compute costs rise. The system that routes tasks between agents becomes the control point, not the models themselves. Banks building AI strategy around model selection may be optimising for the wrong variable. 3 Social Media Addiction Verdicts Redraw Platform Liability for All Digital Services A Los Angeles jury found Meta and YouTube liable for designing addictive platforms that harmed a child user, concluding that features like infinite scroll and recommendation algorithms contributed to addiction. The verdict bypassed Section 230 by framing the claim as product liability (design defect) rather than content liability. Casey Newton argues the distinction between content and design is crucial. Why this matters The verdicts establish that platform design features can be treated as defective products under liability law, independent of content. This precedent will extend to any digital service with algorithmic recommendation, notification systems, or engagement-optimising features, including financial services apps. Banks designing customer-facing AI int

entries.headline: Three Things to Know - 2 April 2026
entries.text: The 60-Second Brief from Disruption Watch Three Things to Know Today 2 April 2026 1 OpenAI Closes Record £92B ($122B) Round as Enterprise Revenue Overtakes Consumer OpenAI confirmed the largest single venture raise in history: £92B ($122B) led by Amazon, Nvidia, and SoftBank, valuing the company at £645B ($852B). Enterprise customers now account for over 40% of revenue, inverting the consumer subscription model. The company also announced a unified "superapp" consolidating its product suite. Why this matters The enterprise revenue shift means AI procurement dynamics, not consumer subscriptions, will drive the next phase. Banks evaluating AI partners should note the structural shift in how frontier AI companies generate revenue. 2 Oracle Cuts Up to 30,000 Jobs While Committing £37.9B ($50B) to AI, Markets Reward the Pivot Oracle laid off between 20,000 and 30,000 employees (18% of its workforce) while committing £37.9B ($50B) to AI infrastructure and signing a £227B+ ($300B+) contract with OpenAI. Shares rose 2%, continuing the pattern of markets rewarding AI-driven workforce reduction. Why this matters The pattern of markets rewarding headcount-to-capex substitution is now established across multiple technology companies. This dynamic will reach financial services within 12 to 18 months. 3 Legal AI Raises £530M+ in a Single Cycle as Vertical AI Disruption Reaches Professional Services Two competing AI platforms for legal services raised substantial rounds in the same cycle: Harvey secured £152M ($200M) at an £8.3B ($11B) valuation, while Legora raised £417M ($550M) at £4.2B ($5.5B). Both are building AI systems that automate legal research, contract analysis, and due diligence at a fraction of the cost and time of traditional law firms. Why this matters Legal services is the canary for professional services disruption by vertical AI. If AI can automate complex legal reasoning at scale, the same pattern will follow in compliance, audit, risk assessment, and financial advisory. Banks should watch which professional services their suppliers begin to automate. Full briefing: Issue #32 available in the Disruption Watch hub. This content is for internal use only.

entries.headline: Three Things to Know
entries.text: Disruption Watch - The 60-Second Brief Three Things to Know Today 3 April 2026 1 min read 1 AI Agent Supply Chains Are Already Being Weaponised Reports show that 20% of software packages recommended by AI coding assistants are hallucinated (they do not exist), and attackers are registering those fake names with malicious code. AI agents select vulnerable packages at a rate 50% higher than human developers. Detection systems catch these attacks in 6 minutes versus the industry average of 267 days. Why This Matters Any organisation deploying AI agents needs to treat supply chain security as a first-order risk, not an afterthought. 2 Anthropic Acquires Biotech Startup for £303M ($400M+) Anthropic has acquired wet-lab biotech startup Coefficient Bio in its first move beyond software into biological research. This signals that frontier AI labs now view biology as a capability frontier alongside language and code. Why This Matters If AI labs routinely acquire wet-lab capabilities, drug discovery timelines compress and insurance actuarial models built on historical health data become unreliable. 3 Google Maps Becomes a Direct Commerce Channel, Erasing Booking Platforms Overnight Google's "Ask Maps" Gemini integration turns Maps into a direct AI commerce channel where users can search, compare, and book without ever leaving the app. Booking platforms, review aggregators, and comparison sites are being disintermediated in real time. Once AI reaches sufficiency inside an existing platform with distribution, it becomes a federated layer that locks out competitors and consolidates market structure. The pattern is repeating across Google's product suite. Why This Matters Any business that sits between a platform and a customer is now exposed. When AI collapses search, comparison, and transaction into a single interface, intermediary business models lose their reason to exist. Banks and fintechs relying on aggregator or marketplace distribution should treat this as a direct threat signal. Full briefing: Issue #33 available in the Disruption Watch hub. This content is for internal use only.

entries.headline: Three Things to Know
entries.text: Disruption Watch - The 60-Second Brief Three Things to Know Today 4 April 2026 1 min read 1 Drift Protocol Suffers £216M Hack via Social Engineering, Not Code Exploit North Korean threat actors extracted £216 million ($285 million) from the Solana-based DeFi platform Drift Protocol using social engineering and a fabricated token (CarbonVote), not a cryptographic vulnerability. The attack, attributed to the Lazarus Group by TRM Labs, exploited human-controlled security gates rather than technical ones, succeeding in under 20 minutes. Why This Matters Financial institutions evaluating crypto-native infrastructure now face evidence that billion-pound platforms can be breached without exploiting code. Social engineering is the binding constraint on infrastructure security, not cryptography. 2 Only 3% of US Consumer Households Are Paying for AI Products Bank of America internal data reveals that despite massive adoption of free-tier AI tools, only 3% of US households are paying subscribers to AI services. Gen Z households show 54% year-on-year spending growth, but absolute penetration remains negligible. Over 60% of businesses now allocate 5% or more of tech spending to AI. Why This Matters The consumer AI revenue model remains unproven at scale. The gap between enterprise AI spending (accelerating) and consumer willingness to pay (stalling) suggests the next battleground is converting free users to paying customers. 3 Bank of England Flags Agentic AI Risk in Payments and Markets The Bank of England's Financial Policy Committee (April 2026 record) acknowledged that agentic AI has not yet created systemic risk but warned that risks are "likely to increase, potentially rapidly". The FPC requested further work on agentic AI behaviour in payments and financial markets, signalling regulatory attention is accelerating ahead of deployment. Why This Matters Central bank attention on agentic AI in payments suggests governance frameworks will be imposed before the technology matures. Institutions building agentic payment systems should anticipate pre-emptive regulatory constraints. Full briefing: Issue #34 available in the Disruption Watch hub. This content is for internal use only.

entries.headline: Research Brief: Circle's AI Agent Infrastructure Thesis
entries.text: Research Brief — 5 March 2026 Circle's AI Agent Infrastructure Thesis The convergence of stablecoins, blockchains, and AI agents — thesis, plausibility, competition, and strategic implications 1. The Thesis Circle CEO Jeremy Allaire is making the boldest bet in fintech infrastructure: that billions of autonomous AI agents will need programmable digital dollars to function, and that USDC on Circle's infrastructure will become the default settlement layer for machine-to-machine commerce. Allaire's core claim (Q4 2025 earnings): "We are entering a world where tens or even hundreds of billions of AI agents will interact and perform economic functions over the Internet. They'll need programmable digital dollars and open infrastructure to do it." This isn't just narrative positioning — Circle is building three concrete infrastructure layers to make it real: Arc (Layer-1 Blockchain) Purpose-built for financial settlement. Public testnet launched October 2025 with 100+ companies, 166M transactions, ~500ms finality, near 100% uptime. Mainnet expected 2026. Allaire calls it "an economic operating system for the internet." Nanopayments (March 2026) Launched on testnet this week. Minimum transaction: $0.000001 (one-millionth of a dollar). Zero gas fees after initial deposit. Batches hundreds of micro-transactions into single blockchain settlement. Built for x402 protocol compliance. Circle Gateway Enables agents to autonomously execute cross-chain USDC transactions at ~$0.00001 per transaction. Full programmability for agent-controlled spending. Integrates with x402 protocol for discover-price-pay-settle workflow. OpenClaw Ecosystem $30K USDC hackathon where AI agents managed everything — submissions, voting, judging, fund distribution. 200+ submissions, 9,700+ comments. Winners built agent wallet infrastructure and AI-only governance systems. 2. The Numbers Behind It Circle's Q4 2025 earnings show the thesis is backed by real traction, not just ambition: Metric Value Growth USDC in circulation ~$75 billion +72% YoY On-chain USDC volume ~$12 trillion +247% YoY Revenue $2.7 billion +64% YoY Adjusted EBITDA $582 million +104% YoY 2026 USDC growth target +40% → ~$105B circulation Arc testnet participants 100+ companies Banking, payments, AI The on-chain volume growth (247% YoY) is the most telling signal — it suggests USDC is increasingly being used for settlement and programmable payments, not just speculation. 3. How AI Agent Payments Actually Work The dominant technical standard is x402 — an extension of HTTP itself that makes payments as simple as loading a web page: x402 Protocol flow: Agent requests resource → Server returns HTTP 402 "Payment Required" with price metadata → Agent authorises stablecoin payment on-chain → Agent resubmits request with payment proof → Server grants access. No API keys, no subscriptions, no billing accounts. Each payment is stateless and independent. The x402 protocol has already processed 35M+ transactions and $10M+ volumes ,
